sources: RFF: How Long Does It Take? (2025)

sectors.name: Solar

metrics.capacityNote: Only 10% of total U.S. solar increase 2010-2023

sectors.keyFinding: Fastest EIS completion of any energy sector (27 months), but 32% cancellation rate and only 10% of solar capacity goes through NEPA

sectors.name: Wind

metrics.capacityNote: Only 3.7% of total U.S. wind increase 2010-2023

sectors.keyFinding: 50% litigation rate for EIS projects — highest of any clean energy sector. 50% meet 2-year target vs. 67% for solar

sectors.name: Transmission
sectors.keyFinding: Average 10-year timeline, with ~80% of time spent waiting for approval. Lowest cancellation rate (12%) suggests projects that survive are resilient

sectors.name: Geothermal

metrics.multipleReviewsNote: A single geothermal project can require up to 6 separate NEPA reviews

sectors.keyFinding: EAs perform well (92% meet 1-year target), but single projects face up to 6 sequential NEPA reviews for different phases

sectors.name: Highways & Transportation
sectors.keyFinding: FHWA EISs take 6.2 years on average — longest of any major agency. Highway costs per mile tripled post-NEPA. 33 states have rejected federal highway funding to bypass NEPA
sectors.caveat: Cost increase is correlation, not proven causation

sectors.name: Public Lands & Forestry
sectors.keyFinding: 37% of ALL NEPA litigation targets public lands projects. Forest Service produces the most EISs (18% of total) and takes 5.1 years on average

sectors.name: Pipelines
sectors.keyFinding: 50% litigation rate but only 22% cancellation — pipeline projects tend to survive legal challenges

sectors.name: Mining & Minerals
sectors.keyFinding: 9% of NEPA litigation with a 76% agency win rate — lower than other sectors, suggesting mining reviews face tougher legal scrutiny

sectors.name: Water Infrastructure
sectors.keyFinding: Army Corps handles 12% of EISs. 85% agency win rate in litigation — highest of any sector
